coredata.dc:title: Lignin-based carbon dots as high-performance support of Pt single atoms for photocatalytic H2 evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuang, Jiandong
Ren, Shiming
Zhu, Bowen
Han, Chunhui
Li, YaoYao
Zhang, Xuexia
Gao, Haili
Fan, Mizi
Tian, Qinfen
coredata.dc:description: Single-atom metal species always run the risk of aggregating into large particles, thus it is essential to develop an appropriate support for Pt single atoms to prevent their aggregation on the premise of ensuring catalytic activity. Herein, N-doped lignin-based carbon dots (NLCDs) prepared by a facile hydrothermal method is used as the support of Pt single atoms for the first time. Through mild photo-assistant method, isolated Pt atoms can bond with the Pyridinic N of NLCDs to form Pt-N4 coordination structures. Moreover, the enhanced π → π* and n → π* electron transitions in Pt-NLCDs make more electrons participate in H2-evolution. The Pt-NLCDs@CdS ternary photocatalyst shows an exceptional visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2-evolution (PHE) rate up to ∼ 46.10 mmol·h−1·g−1. Notably, the ternary system shows ultrahigh photostability in the 60 h continuous photolysis application. Photoelectrochemical analyses demonstrate the synergetic effect of Pt single atoms, NLCDs and CdS will boost the separation, transport and reaction of photogenerated carriers.

head.ce:title: Lignin-based carbon dots as high-performance support of Pt single atoms for photocatalytic H2 evolution

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally and are cofirst authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-atom metal species always run the risk of aggregating into large particles, thus it is essential to develop an appropriate support for Pt single atoms to prevent their aggregation on the premise of ensuring catalytic activity. Herein, N-doped lignin-based carbon dots (NLCDs) prepared by a facile hydrothermal method is used as the support of Pt single atoms for the first time. Through mild photo-assistant method, isolated Pt atoms can bond with the Pyridinic N of NLCDs to form Pt-N4 coordination structures. Moreover, the enhanced π → π* and n → π* electron transitions in Pt-NLCDs make more electrons participate in H2-evolution. The Pt-NLCDs@CdS ternary photocatalyst shows an exceptional visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2-evolution (PHE) rate up to ∼ 46.10 mmol·h−1·g−1. Notably, the ternary system shows ultrahigh photostability in the 60 h continuous photolysis application. Photoelectrochemical analyses demonstrate the synergetic effect of Pt single atoms, NLCDs and CdS will boost the separation, transport and reaction of photogenerated carriers.

ce:section.ce:para: As an environment-friendly and efficient energy, hydrogen (H2) is considered to be the most promising fuel to alleviate the energy crisis, and its production can be realized through a variety of strategies [1–3]. Among them, solar driven photocatalytic water-splitting technology as a specific green way to collect H2 has aroused great interests [4–6]. So far, various sulfides (represented by CdS) have been developed for visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2-evolution, but self-photocorrosion and low quantum efficiency still seriously limit their practical application in water photolysis [7–10]. To retard the recombination of photogenerated carriers and promote their surface proton reduction rates, loading noble-metals on the surface of semiconductor photocatalysts is one of the most effective means [11–14]. Due to the low Fermi level position and suitable H atom adsorption free energy, platinum (Pt) has been proved to be an efficient cocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (PHE) reaction. For the traditional Pt nanoparticles, however, only the outermost Pt atoms can participate in the surface catalytic reaction, most of the Pt atoms are buried in the particle and wasted [15]. The size of Pt nanoparticles thus becomes one of the most significant factors to determine the catalytic activity of supported Pt. Besides that, considering the scarcity and high cost of Pt, tremendous efforts have been devoted toward the most effective use of Pt by downsizing the nanoparticles [16–18].

Single-atom catalyst (SAC), which contains isolated metal active centers atomically dispersed on supports, is an ideal state of supported metal catalyst. Obviously, SACs can extremely reduce the use of expensive and rare metals and maximize their catalytic efficiency [19,20]. However, because of the undesirable aggregation trend of single atoms during the synthesis and catalysis process, manufacturing such highly dispersed and stable SACs is the key for its practical application, and still a major challenge [21–23]. This difficulty stems from the need of an appropriate support that strongly interacts with the isolated single metal atoms to inhibit both aggregation and transformation on the premise of ensuring their catalytic activity. Therefore, it is crucial and necessary to develop an appropriate support which can provide the defined anchoring sites for the isolated Pt single atoms and further investigate their application performance in the field of practical catalysis.

Various supports including mesoporous alumina [24] and ceria [25], carbon nitride [26], nitrogen doped graphene [27], and nitrogen doped carbon dots [18,28] have been reported for anchoring Pt single atoms. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted particular concern due to its advantages of easy preparation, low cost, excellent stability, nontoxicity, high quantum yield and fast electron conduction [29–32]. There are a wide range of carbon sources for preparing CDs, such as carbon fiber, graphene, citric acid, ascorbic acid, vitamins, glucose, lignin and so on [33,34]. Lignin is a three-dimensional network branched phenolic polymer existing in the terrestrial plants. As the only renewable aromatic feedstock, conversion of lignin to high-value chemicals and materials has always been a research hotspot in the field of biomass research. Recently, lignin has been proved to be one of the ideal carbon sources for carbon dots due to its high carbon content and aromatic conjugated structure [35–38]. Furthermore, with endogenous heteroatom and functional groups, the doped or chemically modified carbon dots can be easily synthesized from lignin [39]. The modified carbon dots facilitate the stable dispersion of metal atoms, and thus are expected to act as a promising candidate for supporting single Pt atoms.

Herein, we try to synthesize N-doped LCDs (NLCDs) by a facile hydrothermal method with alkali lignin (AL) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as carbon source and passivator, respectively. Due to the abundant aromatic conjugated structure of lignin, the as-synthesized lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) are expected to achieve well graphitization and thus possess excellent conductivity. Additionally, during the graphitization process, the passivator EDA can provide sufficient N atoms to realize the N-doping of LCDs. Then, through a liquid phase photoreduction method, the isolated Pt atoms can be anchored in situ on the surface of NLCDs by Pt-N bonds to form Pt-NLCDs SAC. Finally, using Pt-NLCDs SAC as cocatalyst, a novel Pt-NLCDs@CdS ternary composite can be successfully constructed. It is found that the synergistic effect of Pt single atoms, NLCDs and CdS can promote the harvest of visible light, improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, increase the active site for H2 evolution, and thus significantly enhance the photocatalytic H2-evolution (PHE) efficiency.

All reagents and the chemical materials are used without further treatment. Alkali lignin (AL) is purchased from Solaibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, EDA), Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O), Hexahydrate chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O), Thiourea (CH4N2S) and Lactic acid (C3H6O3) are received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water is used in all the experiments (resistivity > 18 MΩ∙cm).

NLCDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 0.5 g AL and 2 g EDA were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. After slowly adding 10 mL H2O2 and stirring for another 30 min, the homogeneous solution was sealed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclaves were held at 190 ℃ for 12 h and then cooled down to the room temperature naturally. The brown supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and then filtered through the 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the NLCDs solution (∼28.6 mg/mL).

Pt-NLCDs was fabricated by a liquid-phase photoreduction method. 1 g/L chloroplatinic acid solution (0.3766 g/L of Pt) was prepared in advance, and photon source was the 300 W Xe-lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Perfect Light Co.) with an optical filter of 420 nm. Under vigorous stirring, 25 mL chloroplatinic acid solution was dropwise added into the 25 mL NLCDs (28.6 mg/mL) solution. Then the mixture was illuminated for 10 min to synthesize the Pt-NLCDs solution, in which the theoretical content of Pt is about 2.63 wt%. For comparison, Pt-NLCDs with various Pt contents of 1.60 wt%, 2.12 wt%, 3.14 wt% and 3.65 wt% were also prepared by changing the dosage of chloroplatinic acid, and denoted as Pt-x-NLCDs (x means the theoretical content wt% of Pt). Unless otherwise specified, the “Pt-NLCDs” mentioned in this work refers to the “Pt-2.63-NLCDs”.

1D CdS NRs were synthesized by a solvothermal method [40]. Typically, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (4.6 g) and CH4N2S (6.2 g) were dissolved into 60 mL EDA. After continuous 30 min stirring, the homogenous mixture is transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and held at 160 °C for 50 h. After cooling, the precipitates were gathered by centrifugation and washed with absolute ethanol and water, then dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain CdS NRs.

0.8 mL Pt-NLCDs solution was diluted with ultrapure water to 10 mL, and then added 50 mg of CdS NRs under ultrasonic conditions. After 12 h additional stirring, the mixed solution was centrifuged and dried to obtain the Pt-NLCDs@CdS composite, in which the theoretical Pt content was calculated to be ∼ 0.269 wt% and NLCDs content ∼ 10 wt%.

The typical fabrication processes of CdS, NLCDs, Pt-NLCDs and Pt-NLCDs@CdS are schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 . For reference purpose, the Pt-x-NLCDs@CdS composites with different Pt contents were prepared under the same conditions by using various Pt-x-NLCDs as cocatalyst. Moreover, by changing the volume of Pt-NLCDs solution (0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6 and 2.4 mL) added, Pt-NLCDs@CdS composites with different Pt-NLCDs contents could also be obtained, and denoted as y-Pt-NLCDs@CdS (y means the volume of Pt-NLCDs). Unless otherwise specified, the “Pt-NLCDs@CdS” refers to the “0.8 mL-Pt-2.63-NLCDs@CdS” in this work.

The morphologies of the photocatalysts were studied by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU-8010), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2F20 S-TWIN) and Aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (AC-HAADF-STEM, FEI TITAN ChemiSTEM). The XRD patterns of products were recorded on a Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku) via CuKα radiation operated at 40 kV and 0.04 A. Raman microscope (HORIBA JY, LabRAM HR Evolution) was used to examine the Raman spectra of NLCDs. The fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorbance of NLCDs and Pt-NLCDs were recorded by GangDong F-280 fluorescence spectrophotometer and MAPADA UV-6300 dual-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer, respectively. The surface functional groups of all materials were studied by Bruker VERTEX 70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). The optical absorption properties of the samples were analyzed using PerkinElmer Lambda 750 ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS) equipped with an integrating sphere attachment. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Varian Cary-Eclipse 500 with an excitation wavelength at 380 nm. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were obtained using a FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments) with a test range 0–100 nm, an excitation using 380 nm light source and monitoring of lifetime at 550 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) characterizations were determined by a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha photoelectron spectroscope system. Actual content of constituent elements was detected by Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700 s).

To understand the details of the interaction between Pt with NLCDs, the study performs the first-principles calculations using the DMol3 program and CASTEP program in Materials Studio. The exchange–correlation functional is treated by the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the PBE parameterization. TS method is used for DFT-D correction to refine non-covalent interaction, such as hydrogen bonding. All systems are fully relaxed with symmetry using the DMol3 program. All-electron treatment and double numerical basis including d- and p-polarization function (DNP) are used. The relaxation of atomic positions is considered to be converged, when the change in total energy is less than 1.0 × 10−5 Ha/Å. Partial density of states is analyzed by energy calculation using CASTEP program.

Electrodes were prepared as follows. 5 mg photocatalyst powder was dispersed into 200 μL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and 20 μL Nafion reagent under ultrasonication. Then the mixture was coated onto a 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm FTO glass electrode to serve as working electrode, while the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and Pt as the counter electrode. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested in a K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]/KCl aqueous solution ranging from 100,000 to 0.01 Hz on a ZENNIUM electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Germany). 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was utilized as electrolyte for photocurrent tests with a 300 W Xe-lamp with a cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm) on an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E, Shanghai Chenhua Company, China).

The PHE performance of photocatalysts was evaluated on a commercial reaction system (Lab-solar 6A, Perfect Light Co.) under visible-light irradiation. Typically, in a Pyrex top irradiation quartz reaction reactor, 10 mg of the as-synthesized sample was dispersed into 50 mL solution containing 10 vol% lactic acid as sacrificial reagent. A 300 W Xe-lamp accompanied with an optical filter of 420 nm was used as the visible-light source to vertically irradiate the reaction vessel to generate hydrogen. The reaction vessel was degassed and then maintained at 4 °C by a recirculated cooling system. The produced H2 was detected every 1 h by an online gas chromatography (GC7806) with Argon as the carrier gas. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was determined under the same reaction condition with irradiation using 450 nm narrow band-pass filter. The AQE was calculated using the following formula:
AQE =
2 × n u m b e r o f e v o l v e d
H 2
m o l e c u l e s
numberofincidentphotons
× 100 %

The morphologies of the as-synthesized NLCDs and Pt-NLCDs are characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1
a, the TEM image and the corresponding particle size distribution of NLCDs reveals the large-scale formation of uniform spherical carbon quantum dots of predominantly 1.5–2.8 nm in diameter, and the average particle size is 2.16 nm. Moreover, it is found from the corresponding HRTEM image (Fig. 1 b) that the clear fringes with interplanar distances of 0.34 nm can be matched well with the lattice spacing of (002) planes of ideal graphitic carbon [29,41], confirming the graphitization of lignin macromolecule under hydrothermal condition. This result also agrees well with the XRD pattern of NLCDs ( Figure S1 ). The Raman spectrum of NLCDs (Fig. 1 c) shows that the integral intensity ratio of the D band to the G band is about 0.48, suggesting the high graphitization degree of NLCDs. It is well known that highly graphitized carbon dots usually show good conductivity, which is conducive to the interfacial charge transfer in carbon dots matrix composites.

Fig. 1 d and 1e are the TEM and HRTEM images of Pt-NLCDs, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 1 d that there is no significant change in the morphology and size distribution of Pt-NLCDs compared with NLCDs. More importantly, we can still only observe the lattice fringes of graphitized carbon dots from the HRTEM image (Fig. 1 e), while the corresponding energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum shown in Fig. 1 f proves the coexistence of C, N, O and Pt elements in Pt-NLCDs. The EDX spectrum coupled with HRTEM image reveal that Pt does not exist in the form of nanoparticles, but in the form of small cluster or even isolated atoms on the surface of NLCDs.

AC-HAADF-STEM characterizations are further conducted to verify the dispersion states of Pt species on NLCDs. The high-resolution AC-HAADF-STEM images (Fig. 2
a and 2b) show that countless ultrasmall bright spots with sizes mainly range from 0.1 to 0.2 nm are randomly distributed on the substrate, suggesting that the Pt species exist in the form of isolated single atoms. In order to ascertain the valence state of Pt species and their binding behavior on the surface of NLCDs, the surface chemical states of the as-synthesized NLCDs and Pt-NLCDs are further analyzed by XPS, and the results are compared in Fig. 2 c-e. To compensate for surface charge effects, all binding energies in this work were referenced to the neutral C1s peak (284.6 eV). Consistent with the EDX result, the XPS survey spectrum of Pt-NLCDs in Fig. 2 c clearly shows the coexistence of C, O, N and Pt elements in Pt-NLCDs. The high-resolution C 1 s and O 1 s spectra ( Figure S2
a and 2b) reveal the existence of carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), and amino groups (–NH2) [33,42]. These data further prove that NLCDs have rich surface functional groups, which are consistent well with the FTIR result ( Figure S3 ). As depicted in the FTIR spectrum of NLCDs, there is a smooth wide absorption band in the range of 3000 ∼ 3300 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H bond of secondary amine. While for the Pt-NLCDs, this absorption band is weakened, which is mainly attributed to the strong interaction between Pt and the nitrogen-rich functional groups on the surface of NLCDs. Fig. 2 d is the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of Pt-NLCDs and NLCDs. The wide and asymmetric N 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three dominant peaks located at 398.53 eV, 399.26 eV and 400.67 eV, which can be attributed to Pyridinic N, Pyrrolic N and Graphitic N, respectively [43–45]. Compared with that of NLCDs, as depicted, the Pyridinic N peak intensity of Pt-NLCDs decreases significantly, and the peak position shifts slightly to the high-energy direction by 0.06 eV. These changes are mainly caused by the interaction between Pt ions and Pyridinic N, the forming Pt-N bonds can also make the adsorbed Pt atoms more stable on the carbocyclic ring.

The high-resolution Pt 4f spectrum of Pt-NLCDs is further analyzed to clarify the chemical states of Pt element. As shown in Fig. 2 e, two sets of peaks are obtained by fitting the high-resolution Pt 4f spectrum, which can be attributed to the characteristic peaks of Pt2+ 4f7/2/4f5/2 (72.91 eV and 76.20 eV) and Pt4+ 4f7/2/4f5/2 (74.83 eV and 78.07 eV) [46,47], respectively. The oxidation state of Pt4+ mainly comes from the PtCl6 2− ions directly adsorbed on NLCDs, while the low oxidation state (Pt2+) can be explained by the interaction between PtCl6 2− with the electron-donating Pyridinic N. It is noteworthy that the integral area of Pt2+ peaks are much higher than that of Pt4+ peaks, indicating that a substantial part of the adsorbed PtCl6 2− can interact with Pyridinic N to form Pt-N bonds [48–51]. In line with the HRTEM results, no metallic Pt element can be detected in the spectrum, confirming that the mild visible-light irradiation during the photoreduction process would not cause the excessive reduction, aggregation and growth of Pt elements.

Fig. 3
a compares the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NLCDs and Pt-NLCDs. It is found that the K-band absorption (π → π*, conjugated structure) and R-band absorption (n → π*, C = N) of Pt-NLCDs are fairly enhanced in the wavelength region of 225–300 nm, suggesting a strong interaction between Pt and N-doped NLCDs. According to the recent studies reported by An [52] and Su [53], the enhanced π → π* and n → π* electron transition have a positive contribution to enhancing photocatalytic activity. Additionally, a notable fluorescence quenching effect can also be observed in the Pt decorated sample (Fig. 3 b), implying the anchored Pt species can promote the interfacial transfer of photoexcited electrons in NLCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of Pt atoms on N-doped LCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Furthermore, six different structural models of Pt-NLCDs were established to explore the details of the interaction between NLCDs and Pt. As shown in Fig. 4 and Figure S4
, C240 is used to represent NLCDs, and six different N-doping types in C240 are considered. The first N-doping type is that four N atoms replace four C atoms to form nitrogen-centric vacant defects (Pyridinic N), while the remaining five types are as follows: a) substitutional defect in which four N atoms replace four C atoms, b) substitutional defect in which one N atom replaces one C atom (Graphitic N), c) four O atoms replace four C atoms to form oxygen-centric vacant defects, d) epoxy structure with two C-O bonds, e) substitutional defect in which one O atom replaces one C atom. The simulation results show that only in the structural model of Pyridinic N, the Pt atoms can form stable Pt-N4 coordination structure with the doped N atoms through the Pt-N bonds (Fig. 4 a-b), while no new bonds can be formed in the other five models ( Figure S4
a-e). This result further verifies that Pt ions will bond with the Pyridinic N and be firmly anchored to NLCDs in the photoreduction process. In addition, the partial density of states (DOS) of Pt-NLCDs were also recorded (Fig. 4 c-e) to clear the electronic properties in the composite. It is found that the DOS of Pt-NLCDs at Fermi level are mainly provided by the N-doped NLCDs, and the 3d electrons of Pt make a small contribution to the electronic states. This coordination structure can make the loaded Pt single atoms accumulate more negative polarized charges, and upshift its d-band centers toward the Fermi level, which is favor the dissociation of H2 [54].

Based on the above analysis, a conjecture of the incorporation of isolated Pt atoms on the NLCDs supports is proposed. For the N-doped LCDs prepared by hydrothermal method, there are a large number of active functional groups on its surface, which can act as active sites for chemical adsorption of PtCl6 2− ions. Under the irradiation of mild visible-light, Pt ions can interact with Pyridinic N to form Pt-N bonds and Pt-N4 coordination structure. This coordination structure can firmly confine the Pt atoms to realize the stable anchoring of isolated Pt atoms on the surface of NLCDs, and make it accumulate more negative polarized charges. Moreover, the Pt-NLCDs cocatalysts can show enhanced π → π* and n → π* electron transitions due to the strong interaction between Pt atoms and NLCDs. The negative charged Pt sites and enhanced electron transitions will be conducive to improving photocatalytic H2 evolution activity.

The morphologies of CdS and its composites are examined by FESEM ( Figure S5 ) and TEM (Fig. 5 ). As shown in Fig. 5 a-b and
Figure S5 , the as-synthesized CdS is of one-dimensional nanorod structure, and loading NLCDs or Pt-NLCDs has little effect on its morphology. Moreover, it is obvious in Fig. 5 c that the dispersed Pt-NLCDs nanoparticles are adhered uniformly to the surface of CdS NRs. While the clear fringes observed in the corresponding HRTEM image (Fig. 5 d) can be assigned to the lattice spacing of hexagonal CdS (100) plane (0.36 nm) and graphitic carbon (002) plane (0.338 nm), respectively. Similarly, in this image, we still cannot find the lattice fringes representing Pt, confirming the loading process will not cause the reaggregation of isolated Pt atoms. The SAED pattern (Fig. 5 e) of Pt-NLCDs@CdS reveals a standard single crystal diffraction pattern of hexagonal CdS, while the impurity spots can be assigned to the loaded carbon dots.

The HAADF-STEM image of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is shown in Fig. 5 f, and Fig. 5 g is the corresponding EDS element mapping images in the rectangular region selected by Fig. 5 f. It can be seen that Cd, S, Pt and C elements are uniformly distributed on CdS NRs, corresponding to the EDX spectrum of Pt-NLCDs@CdS ( Figure S6 ). The scattered Pt and C elements on the copper mesh film are mainly caused by the strong ultrasonic dispersion during the preparation of TEM sample. Moreover, although the theoretical loading content of Pt in Pt-NLCDs@CdS is calculated to be ∼ 0.269 wt%, the actual loading of Pt is determined to be only 0.06 wt% by ICP-MS (Table S1).

In order to investigate the effect of Pt-NLCDs cocatalyst on the surface chemical states of CdS, the samples were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 6
a displays the fully scanned XPS spectra of all samples in the range of 0 ∼ 700 eV. Due to the low loading amount of Pt and the overlap of Cd 4p peak with Pt 4f peak, only C, Cd and S elements other than Pt can be found in the survey spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 b, loading NLCDs-based cocatalysts will dramatically enhance the peak intensity of C 1 s, and the wide and asymmetric C 1 s spectra can be convoluted into three peaks. For NLCDs-CdS, the main C 1 s peak (284.6 eV) is dominated by graphitic carbon element, while two shoulder peaks at 285.56 eV and 287.98 eV can be assigned to C-N and C-O bonds, respectively [53,55]. It can also be found that the shoulder peak intensity of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is much weaker than that of NLCDs@CdS, meaning the successful loading of Pt on NLCDs surface. In addition, the positive shift (∼0.2 eV) of C-N bond implies that the doped N-sites are the main anchoring position of Pt single atoms with high electron affinity. This analysis can also be confirmed by the FTIR results ( Figure S9 ).

Moreover, it is observed from Fig. 6 c that the binding energies of S 2p are located at 161.01 and 162.21 eV in CdS, which can be attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. Loading NLCDs will shift the binding energies of S 2p towards higher energy, indicating an obvious interaction between NLCDs and CdS. Meanwhile, for Pt-NLCDs@CdS, the S 2p core lines shift negatively by ∼ 0.15 eV and ∼ 0.16 eV, which could be ascribed to the electronic interaction between Pt-N bonds with S (by forming Pt-S bonds at the interface of Pt-NLCDs and CdS) [17,23]. By fitting the Cd 3d high-resolution spectra (Fig. 6 d), two characteristic Cd2+ peaks at 404.47 eV (Cd 3d5/2) and 411.21 eV (Cd 3d3/2) can be obtained [40,56]. Compared with that of pure CdS, the Cd 3d core lines of NLCDs@CdS and Pt-NLCDs@CdS show the same shift trend as S 2p. These results demonstrate that Pt-NLCDs loading will increase the surface electron density of CdS[16,17], leading to remarkable negative shifts of Cd 3d and S 2p core lines.

In order to evaluate the performance of photocatalytic H2 evolution (PHE), the synthesized photocatalysts were measured under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) with 10 vol% lactic acid as the sacrificial agent on a commercial photocatalytic reaction system. Fig. 7
a summaries the PHE rate of CdS, various cocatalysts and their composites. As depicted, the PHE activity of individual cocatalysts (NLCDs or Pt-NLCDs) is negligible, while that of pure CdS NRs is tested to be ∼ 0.67 mmol·h−1·g−1. After loading of cocatalysts, the PHE performance of CdS is fairly promoted, and the H2 evolution rate is determined to be ∼ 3.18 mmol·h−1·g−1 for NLCDs@CdS and ∼ 15.68 mmol·h−1·g−1 for Pt@CdS. This result suggests that although its cocatalysis is weaker than that of Pt, the high conductive NLCDs can also act as a good cocatalyst to enhance the interface transfer of the charge carriers generated in CdS. Notably, when Pt-NLCDs is used as the cocatalyst, the Pt-NLCDs@CdS composites shows an ultrahigh PHE activity of ∼ 46.10 mmol·h−1·g−1 ( Video S1 ), which is approximately 68.8, 14.5 and 2.9 times that of pure CdS NRs, NLCDs@CdS and Pt@CdS, respectively. This amazing promotion can be attributed to the synergistic effects between high conductive NLCDs and high active Pt single atoms.

In addition, to clear the effect of different types of CDs on the synergistic effects with Pt single atoms, we also synthesized LCDs (from lignin without N-doping) and various N-doped carbon dots (NCCDs from citric acid, NGCDs from glucose, and NChCDs from chitosan) by the same route with NLCDs. As shown in Fig. 7 b, the promotion effects of various carbon dots on the PHE activities are in the descending order of Pt-NLCDs > Pt-NGCDs ≈ Pt-LCDs > Pt-NChCDs > Pt-NCCDs. It is deduced that among these biomaterials, lignin is of abundant aromatic conjugated structure and highest carbon content, thus can form high conductive carbon quantum dots to maximize the synergistic effect with Pt single atoms. Moreover, the PHE performance of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is ∼ 1.42 times higher than that of Pt-LCDs@CdS. By comparing the actual Pt loading amount of Pt-NLCDs and Pt-LCDs (Table S1), it is found that the N-doping can enhance the Pt-loading capacity of carbon dots, thus showing better cocatalysis performance.

The effects of Pt contents and Pt-NLCDs contents on the PHE rate of the composites were also investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 c and 7d. With the increase of Pt content, the PHE rate of the composites raises first and then decrease, and the best Pt content of Pt-NLCDs is determined to be ∼ 2.63 wt% (Fig. 7 c). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 7 d, blindly increasing the loading amount of Pt-NLCDs cocatalyst cannot continuously improve the PHE activity of Pt-NLCDs@CdS, because the light-block effect of cocatalyst on CdS enhances with the increase of loading content. Thus, the optimal loading amount of cocatalyst can also be determined, and the theoretic content of NLCDs and Pt in Pt-NLCDs@CdS is calculated to be ∼ 10 wt% and ∼ 0.269 wt%, respectively.

Furthermore, the wavelength-dependent AQE values of Pt-NLCDs@CdS (25 mg) are also estimated in Fig. 7 e. After 4 h irradiation, the H2 evolution amounts was estimated to be 1.128 mmol (∼0.282 mmol/h) under 450 nm wavelength irradiation. According to equation of
N
photons
=
E λ
hc
, the AQE at λ = 450 nm is calculated to be 9.79 %. With the same method, the PHE rates of Pt-NLCDs@CdS at 420 nm, 450 nm, 500 nm, and 550 nm is determined to be 6.74, 11.27, 11.68, and 0.33 mmol/h∙g, respectively. If combine the wavelength-dependent PHE rate with the UV-DRS spectrum, it can be found that the wavelength-depended PHE rate of Pt-NLCDs@CdS qualitatively tracks its characteristic absorption spectrum. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, the visible-light driven PHE performance of Pt-NLCDs@CdS reported in this work is superior than most of those obtained by CdS loaded with Pt-cocatalyst (Table S2).

This enhancement and high stability may be due to the following factors: i) the heterojunction between high conductive NLCDs and CdS NRs will facilitate the efficient transport and separation of photogenerated carriers; ii) Pt has a very high work function (∼5.36 eV) and can easily capture electrons generated by the photoexcited NLCDs and CdS NRs; iii) the electrons can quickly react with protons to evolute hydrogen.

In order to ascertain the source of the enhancement effect of the Pt-NLCDs cocatalyst, the optical and electrochemical properties of the samples are characterized in detail. Fig. 8
a shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of various samples as well as the digital photos of CdS and Pt-NLCDs@CdS. Compared with pristine CdS NRs, the fundamental absorption edge of Pt-NLCDs@CdS shows an obvious redshift, implying hybrid interfacial structures are formed between Pt-NLCDs and CdS NRs. Additionally, the existence of cocatalyst can enhance the visible-light adsorption of composites in the region with wavelength ≥ 525 nm. These changes in DRS spectra are also clearly reflected in the color change of specimens from bright yellow to greenish yellow before and after loading Pt-NLCDs. Based on the Kubelka-Munk method, the band gaps of pure CdS and Pt-NLCDs@CdS are determined to be ∼ 2.43 eV and ∼ 2.42 eV, respectively (Fig. 8 b). The electronic band structure of the Pt-NLCDs@CdS composite can be further estimated by the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). As shown in Fig. 8 c, the work function of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is −4.54 eV in vacuum scale, while the valence band maximum (VBM, −6.10 eV) is ∼ 1.56 eV lower than the corresponding Fermi level. By combining the bandgap energy and UPS valence band spectra, the corresponding band structure of Pt-NLCDs@CdS can be established in Fig. 8 d.

To further investigate the charge separation and transfer efficiency of the as-prepared samples, photoelectrochemical analyses including transient photocurrent response, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique were also performed. It is found in Fig. 9
a that the ternary Pt-NLCDs@CdS shows the highest visible-light induced photocurrent, meaning the largest density of photoexcited charges. Moreover, compared with pure CdS NRs and NLCDs@CdS, Pt-NLCDs@CdS also displays faster transient photocurrent response speed and more stable current intensity, revealing that the photogenerated carriers in Pt-NLCDs@CdS have higher separation rate and more stable separation efficiency.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers can also be reflected by the PL emission signal intensity [57,58]. As is observed in Fig. 9 b, under the excitation of λex = 380 nm, all the samples show a PL emission band at 500–700 nm at room temperature. The PL intensity is in a descending order of CdS > NLCDs@CdS > Pt-NLCDs@CdS, and the significantly suppressed PL signal of Pt-NLCDs@CdS can be regarded as the remarkable enhancement in the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. This deduction can be proved by the TRPL decay spectra (Fig. 9 c). The decay kinetics are fitted with a two-exponential decay function, and the results are summarized in Table S3. As depicted, the Pt-NLCDs@CdS (36.76 ns) shows a shorter PL lifetime than that of NLCDs@CdS (50.36 ns) and pristine CdS (52.68 ns). According to the reports of Qiu[17] and Luo[18], the reduced PL lifetime suggests an ultrafast electron transfer from CdS to the Pt-NLCDs, mainly caused by the electron accepting ability of NLCDs and the high electron affinity of Pt single atoms[59].

The EIS Nyquist plots were also carefully investigated to understand the interfacial transfer ability of the charge carriers in various samples. As shown in Fig. 9 d, the Nyquist plots of all three samples display semi-cycles at high frequencies. The plots at the high frequency reflects the charge transfer limiting process of the system, which is mainly attributed to the charge transfer resistance between electrode and electrolyte [60]. The smaller radius arc at high frequency thus corresponds to a higher charge transfer rate across the electrode–electrolyte interface [61]. Among these three samples, the smallest arc radius of Pt-NLCDs@CdS signifies its best interfacial charge transfer efficiency, which is consistent with the transient photocurrent response and PL result. Obviously, the interfacial transfer of photogenerated carriers has been significantly improved in the ternary Pt-NLCDs@CdS composite. The electrochemical hydrogen evolution activities of the samples are further investigated by the LSV technique, and the corresponding polarization curves are displayed in Fig. 9 e. Herein, we use the tangent method to determine the inflection point of the polarization curve, and the corresponding potential is defined as the onset overpotential associated with H2 evolution. As depicted, the cathode current density of pristine CdS is very small even at high applied voltage. After decorated with cocatalysts, the cathode current density of the electrodes increases significantly under the same test conditions. The onset potential of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is determined to be −0.201 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), approximate 0.113 V and 0.081 V less than that of pristine CdS and LCDs@CdS, respectively, further proving that the isolated Pt atoms can act as active sites for efficient H2-evolution and effectively reduce the overpotential of H2-evolution reaction [62,63].

Based on the above analysis, the synergistic effects of NLCDs, Pt single atoms and CdS on the efficient separation of photogenerated carriers and the improved PHE performance is proposed, and a reliable synergistic mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 . Thanks to the abundant aromatic conjugated structures and endogenous heteroatom of lignin, the as-prepared N-doped LCDs is an excellent supporter of good conductivity and rich functional surface groups. Under mild photo-assistant condition, isolated Pt atoms can efficiently bond with the Pyridinic N of NLCDs to form stable Pt-N4 coordination structure, which is in favor of the accumulation of electrons on Pt single atoms. In addition, the isolated Pt atoms can effectively reduce the H2 evolution overpotential and act as the active sites for the dissociation of H2O molecules. These virtues of Pt-NLCDs combined with the enhanced π → π* and n → π* electron transitions under irradiation make it an ideal cocatalyst for photocatalytic water splitting. After integrated with CdS, a interaction between Pt-N bonds and S atoms can also be found at the heterojunction, which is also conducive to the interfacial transfer of photogenerated electrons from CdS to Pt-NLCDs.

In summary, using alkali lignin as carbon source and ethylenediamine as passivator, N-doped LCDs are successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. The NLCDs is used as the high-performance supporter of Pt single atoms for the first time, and a novel Pt-NLCDs@CdS nanocomposite has been fabricated for high-efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution. DFT result verifies that Pt atoms can bond with the Pyridinic N of NLCDs to form stable Pt-N4 coordination structure, which is in favor of the accumulation of negative polarized charges on Pt single atoms. In addition, after integrated with CdS, a significant interaction between Pt-N4 coordination structure with S atoms can be formed to facilitate the interfacial transfer of charge carriers between CdS and Pt-NLCDs cocatalyst. With the optimized loading amounts of Pt (0.269 wt%) and NLCDs (10 wt%), the PHE activity of Pt-NLCDs@CdS is up to 46.10 mmol·h−1·g−1, which is 68.8 times higher than that of pristine CdS NRs (∼0.67 mmol·h−1·g−1). More importantly, the ternary photocatalytic system shows an ultrahigh stability in photocatalytic water splitting, and the PHE rate can remain above 38 mmol·h−1·g−1 even after 60 h continuous irradiation. Photoelectrochemical analyses demonstrate that the synergetic effect of Pt single atoms, NLCDs and CdS will boost the separation, transport and reaction of photogenerated charge carriers. This study can not only provide an innovative inspiration for the high-value utilization of lignin, and the rational design of high-performance support for monatomic photocatalysis.
